response.explanation:
Continuing Airworthiness Management Responsibilities

The continuing airworthiness management system (CAMO) is responsible for ensuring that all aircraft remain safe and compliant throughout their operational life. The key responsibilities, drawn from the retrieved material, are organized around the people who carry them out, the systems they use, and the procedures they follow.

| Area | What the CAMO Must Do | Who Is Involved |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Nominated Postholder | • Must have corporate authority to finance and carry out all continuing‑airworthiness activities to the required standard.
• Ensures maintenance is performed on time and to an approved standard.
• Holds Part‑M responsibility for continuing airworthiness. | The nominated postholder (often the same person as the “group of persons” in small operators). |
| Group of Persons | • Covers all duties and responsibilities described in Part‑M.
• Provides a detailed list of job functions that together meet the regulatory requirements. | The nominated postholder and any additional staff designated by the operator. |
| Manpower & Training | • The CAMO must show that the number of people assigned to continuing‑airworthiness tasks is adequate (e.g., a table of full‑time, part‑time, and equivalent full‑time staff).
• Must have a training policy that demonstrates staff are properly trained for their roles. | All staff involved in continuing‑airworthiness activities. |
| Records Management | • Maintain an aircraft continuing‑airworthiness records system and technical log.
• Record flight hours and cycles accurately to plan maintenance tasks.
• Ensure records are retained and accessible. | CAMO personnel responsible for record keeping. |
| Minimum Equipment List (MEL) Procedures | • Issue, update, use, and manage the MEL (if applicable). | CAMO staff handling MEL. |
| Maintenance Programme | • Identify State‑of‑Registry requirements.
• Develop, update, approve, or accept the maintenance programme.
• Identify sources for the programme and mandatory tasks. | CAMO personnel overseeing maintenance programmes. |
| Subcontracting | • Only subcontract tasks that are appropriately approved.
• Have procedures to control subcontracted arrangements.
• Contracts must specify that the subcontractor informs the CAMO (and the competent authority) of any changes affecting performance.
• The CAMO must have access to all relevant data and retain the authority to override subcontractor recommendations if necessary.
• Ensure the subcontractor has qualified technical expertise and sufficient resources. | CAMO and the subcontracted organisation. |
| Authority & Oversight | • The CAMO retains ultimate authority over all continuing‑airworthiness activities.
• Must monitor and accept the subcontractor’s procedures and amendments.
• Contracts should include provisions for competent‑authority monitoring and closure of findings. | CAMO leadership and regulatory authorities. |

Bottom line:
The CAMO’s responsibilities cover the people (nominated postholder, group of persons, trained staff), the systems (records, MEL, maintenance programme), and the processes (manpower allocation, training, subcontracting controls). All of these elements must be documented, monitored, and maintained to meet regulatory standards and ensure the aircraft remain airworthy.
